question: Define a non-seasonal ARIMA(p,d,q) model by clearly explaining the meaning and purpose of each of the three components: p, d, and q.
expectedPoints: p represents the autoregressive (AR) order, indicating how many lagged values of the series are used as predictors.
d represents the degree of differencing (I for Integrated) required to make the time series stationary by removing trends.
q represents the moving average (MA) order, indicating how many lagged error terms are included in the model.

question: Explain the Box-Jenkins methodology for building an ARIMA model. Describe the three main stages involved.
expectedPoints: Model Identification: Use tools like ACF and PACF plots on the (differenced) data to tentatively identify the orders p and q for the AR and MA components.
Parameter Estimation: Use a method like Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) to fit the identified model and estimate its coefficients.
Model Diagnostics: Check the residuals of the fitted model for whiteness (no autocorrelation) to ensure the model has adequately captured the structure in the data.

question: A time series analyst is trying to model a dataset that shows a clear upward trend and changing variance over time. Describe the specific problems these features (trend and heteroskedasticity) pose for standard ARIMA modeling. Then, detail the steps you would take to transform the data and properly specify a model, including how you would determine the appropriate d and how you might address the variance issue.
expectedPoints: Problem: A non-stationary series with a trend violates the mean stationarity assumption of ARIMA, and heteroskedasticity (changing variance) violates the constant variance assumption, leading to unreliable parameter estimates and forecasts.

Solution for Trend: Apply differencing. The appropriate order of differencing (d) is determined by performing unit root tests (e.g., ADF test) or by observing when the ACF plot decays rapidly to zero, indicating stationarity has been achieved.

Solution for Heteroskedasticity: Before ARIMA modeling, apply a variance-stabilizing transformation like the logarithmic or Box-Cox transformation to achieve a constant variance throughout the series.

Final Model Specification: After transformation and differencing, use the stationarized series to identify the AR and MA orders (p and q) using ACF/PACF plots or information criteria, then estimate and diagnose the full ARIMA(p,d,q) model.
